title: Scottish oatcakes
subtitle: Crisp savoury oat crackers for cheese and smoked fish
excerpt: Thin, crisp rounds made from oatmeal, a little flour, lard or butter, and boiling water. No sugar, no leavening, no eggs. The boiling water gelatinises some of the oat starch and makes the dough mouldable despite its low fat content. Eaten with cheese, smoked salmon, or pâté.
sourceNotes: Oatcakes have been the staple bread of Scotland since at least the medieval period, documented by visitors and travellers as the common food of ordinary Scots. Recipes appear in Scottish domestic cookbooks from the eighteenth century onwards, including Elizabeth Cleland, A New and Easy Method of Cookery (1755). The basic formula of oatmeal, fat, salt, and boiling water has remained unchanged. The recipe here follows the traditional formula documented in nineteenth-century Scottish domestic sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed bag once completely cool. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes and crisp briefly in a low oven if needed.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in a tin for up to two weeks. They continue to crisp and dry over the first few days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 150°C. Oatcakes bake slowly; they are done when the surface is dry and the underside is lightly coloured — 20 to 25 minutes. They crisp further as they cool and are not fully crisp until completely cold.

glossaryTerms.definition: A savoury cracker made from oatmeal rather than wheat flour, bound with a small amount of fat and boiling water. The boiling water is essential: it partially gelatinises the oat starch, binding the dough without egg or large quantities of fat. The resulting oatcake is dry and crisp rather than rich or short, with a nutty oat flavour. Roll the dough while warm — it stiffens as it cools and becomes difficult to roll thin.

content.text: is not a cake and shares little with the sweet commercial oatcakes sold in supermarkets. It is a thin, dry, savoury cracker. The dough must be rolled while warm — it stiffens quickly and will crack if left to cool before rolling.

attrs.prepNote: blended to a coarse meal in a food processor

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Mix the blended oats, flour, salt, and bicarbonate of soda in a bowl. Add the lard and rub it in quickly. Pour in the boiling water and mix to a soft, warm dough — add a little more water if the dough is crumbly and will not hold together.

Divide the dough in half. Working quickly while the dough is still warm, roll each half on a lightly floured surface to about 3 mm thickness. Cut into rounds of 7 to 8 cm diameter or into squares and place on the lined trays. Gather the offcuts, re-roll, and cut again. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the surface is dry and the underside is pale gold. They will not colour much on top. Cool completely on a rack — they are not fully crisp until cold.
